### 1D Features

The primary sequence of MoHog1/MoOsm1 (357 aa) exhibits the classic architecture of a MAP kinase. The catalytic domain (residues 23-311) contains all the conserved motifs necessary for phosphotransferase activity. Notably, the activation loop features the TGY motif (T171/Y173), characteristic of the stress-activated protein kinase (SAPK) family [@dixon1999; @liu2020]. The activation of this motif through dual phosphorylation has been experimentally validated under osmotic and oxidative stress conditions, as well as in response to fungicide exposure @motoyama2008. 

Building upon previous structural studies of p38 MAPKs, which demonstrated the formation of dimers through the swapping of activation segments @rothweiler2011, the oligomeric state of MoOsm1 was experimentally evaluated. Functional assays confirmed that MoHog1/MoOsm1 exists in both monomeric and dimeric forms. However, the ability to dimerize is strictly regulated by the phosphorylation state of the activation loop. 

Mutational analysis revealed that phosphorylation at Tyr173 acts as a molecular switch that prevents dimerization, whereas phosphorylation at Thr171 has no effect. These results suggest that phosphorylation at Tyr173 would likely cause steric clashes or structural instability in the dimer. Consistently, the phosphorylated form (simulated by the Y173D mutation) does not dimerize in vivo, indicating that structural models forcing a phosphorylated activation loop into a dimeric interface would probably be energetically unfavorable or distorted [@liu2020; @zhang2024]. 

#### **Solvent accessibility (NetSurfP)**

The solvent accessibility of MoOsm1/MoHog1 were evaluated using the [NetSurfP-3.0](https://services.healthtech.dtu.dk/services/NetSurfP-3.0/) algorithm (@fig-NetSurfP-MoOsm1). 

![**MoOsm1/MoHog1 solvent accesibility prediction by NetSurfP**](/pics/Protein3/NetSurf_aa_chain.png){#fig-NetSurfP-MoOsm1}

The secondary structure prediction revealed a distinct modular organization: the N-terminal half of the sequence is characterized by a high density of $\beta$-sheets (interrupted by a single $\alpha$-helix), whereas the C-terminal half exhibits a predominant representation of $\alpha$-helices, interspersed with unstructured regions categorized as coils. The protein core shows low disorder probability, while significant disorder is present only at the flexible N- and C-terminal ends.

The RSA analysis shows more exposed than buried residues. Importantly, the activation loop containing the TGY phosphorylation motif (residues 171–173) is highly solvent-exposed (above the 25% threshold), ensuring accessibility to MAPKKs and phosphatases and enabling rapid phosphorylation during the fungal stress response.
